findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a large, high-density joint effusion, characteristically seen distending the suprapatellar bursa on a lateral radiograph of the knee. This increased density strongly suggests the presence of blood within the joint space, known as a hemarthrosis [1, 2].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include an associated intra-articular fracture fragment, such as an ossific density along the posterior aspect of the tibial plateau, which is the underlying source of the bleeding. A lipohemarthrosis, indicated by a fat-fluid level, may also be visible on a cross-table lateral view if marrow fat has escaped into the joint capsule [1].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of extensive periarticular osteopenia, epiphyseal overgrowth, or a widened intercondylar notch, which are chronic features that would suggest an underlying coagulopathy like hemophilia. Furthermore, there is no subchondral cyst formation or frank marginal erosion to suggest a chronic inflammatory or synovial proliferative process [1, 2].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a dense suprapatellar effusion combined with a discrete intra-articular fracture fragment [1, 2].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Hemarthrosis secondary to trauma, given the dense joint effusion accompanied by a visible intra-articular fracture fragment. Traumatic hemarthrosis is the most common cause of a dense joint effusion, and its presence mandates a meticulous search for subtle intra-articular fracture extensions or signs of internal derangement [1, 2].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Hemarthrosis (hemophilia, posttraumatic), however patients with hemophilia suffer from recurrent bleeds that lead to chronic secondary sequelae. These long-term changes, such as cartilage damage, multiple subarticular cysts, epiphyseal enlargement, and a widened intercondylar notch, are conspicuously absent in this acute presentation [1, 2].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Juvenile chronic arthritis (JCA), given that missing this condition in a child could delay essential immunosuppressive therapy, leading to irreversible joint destruction and long-term disability. However, while JCA can present with a dense effusion and a widened intercondylar notch, it typically features generalized periarticular osteopenia and lacks the acute fracture fragment seen here [2].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to identify any radiographically occult intra-articular fracture extensions and thoroughly evaluate the internal ligamentous and meniscal structures [1, 2].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine if the patient has a baseline history of joint effusions or prior trauma that might suggest a pre-existing bleeding diathesis.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the knee, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating radiographically occult osteochondral fractures, mapping the exact extent of cartilage damage, and evaluating for associated cruciate ligament or meniscal tears.
management.ctPhases: A non-contrast CT with multiplanar reformats could be considered if the fracture pattern is complex, to aid in preoperative mapping of the tibial plateau or femoral condyle articular surface.
management.mriSequences: I would specify fluid-sensitive sequences such as sagittal and coronal STIR or fat-saturated T2-weighted images to detect subtle bone marrow edema from contusions, alongside high-resolution T1-weighted sequences to precisely evaluate the integrity of the menisci and cruciate ligaments.
management.intervention: If the effusion is tense and exquisitely painful, or if septic arthritis becomes a clinical concern, I would specify an ultrasound-guided joint aspiration to relieve pressure and obtain fluid for urgent microbiological analysis [2].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Orthopaedic and Trauma MDT. My report would clearly document the presence of the traumatic hemarthrosis, the exact location and displacement of the intra-articular fracture fragment, and any suspicion of broader internal derangement to guide urgent surgical fixation or arthroscopic management.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a large, high-density joint effusion, characteristically seen distending the suprapatellar bursa on a lateral radiograph of the knee. This increased density strongly suggests the presence of blood within the joint space, known as a hemarthrosis [1, 2]. Supporting features include an associated intra-articular fracture fragment, such as an ossific density along the posterior aspect of the tibial plateau, which is the underlying source of the bleeding. A lipohemarthrosis, indicated by a fat-fluid level, may also be visible on a cross-table lateral view if marrow fat has escaped into the joint capsule [1]. Importantly, there is no evidence of extensive periarticular osteopenia, epiphyseal overgrowth, or a widened intercondylar notch, which are chronic features that would suggest an underlying coagulopathy like hemophilia. Furthermore, there is no subchondral cyst formation or frank marginal erosion to suggest a chronic inflammatory or synovial proliferative process [1, 2]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a dense suprapatellar effusion combined with a discrete intra-articular fracture fragment [1, 2].

The appearances are most in keeping with Hemarthrosis secondary to trauma, given the dense joint effusion accompanied by a visible intra-articular fracture fragment. Traumatic hemarthrosis is the most common cause of a dense joint effusion, and its presence mandates a meticulous search for subtle intra-articular fracture extensions or signs of internal derangement [1, 2]. The principle differential would be Hemarthrosis (hemophilia, posttraumatic), however patients with hemophilia suffer from recurrent bleeds that lead to chronic secondary sequelae. These long-term changes, such as cartilage damage, multiple subarticular cysts, epiphyseal enlargement, and a widened intercondylar notch, are conspicuously absent in this acute presentation [1, 2]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Juvenile chronic arthritis (JCA), given that missing this condition in a child could delay essential immunosuppressive therapy, leading to irreversible joint destruction and long-term disability. However, while JCA can present with a dense effusion and a widened intercondylar notch, it typically features generalized periarticular osteopenia and lacks the acute fracture fragment seen here [2].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to identify any radiographically occult intra-articular fracture extensions and thoroughly evaluate the internal ligamentous and meniscal structures [1, 2]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine if the patient has a baseline history of joint effusions or prior trauma that might suggest a pre-existing bleeding diathesis. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the knee, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating radiographically occult osteochondral fractures, mapping the exact extent of cartilage damage, and evaluating for associated cruciate ligament or meniscal tears. A non-contrast CT with multiplanar reformats could be considered if the fracture pattern is complex, to aid in preoperative mapping of the tibial plateau or femoral condyle articular surface. I would specify fluid-sensitive sequences such as sagittal and coronal STIR or fat-saturated T2-weighted images to detect subtle bone marrow edema from contusions, alongside high-resolution T1-weighted sequences to precisely evaluate the integrity of the menisci and cruciate ligaments. If the effusion is tense and exquisitely painful, or if septic arthritis becomes a clinical concern, I would specify an ultrasound-guided joint aspiration to relieve pressure and obtain fluid for urgent microbiological analysis [2]. This should go to the Orthopaedic and Trauma MDT. My report would clearly document the presence of the traumatic hemarthrosis, the exact location and displacement of the intra-articular fracture fragment, and any suspicion of broader internal derangement to guide urgent surgical fixation or arthroscopic management.
